narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty days after her April marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Walter married Timothy Bishop on April 3, 1800 and died on May 3, 1800 at age nineteen — about thirty days after the marriage, with no children. The compressed marriage-and-death cycle within a single calendar month was striking even by the period's standards of vulnerability to acute disease. Her husband Timothy lived another seventy-three years and remarried twice.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa is buried at Grove Street Cemetery in New Haven, Plot 11 Cypress Avenue East. The cemetery — one of America's first planned burial grounds and a National Historic Landmark — became the family-burial cluster for the Walter-Pierpont line, with her parents William Jay Walter and Theodosia and several siblings later joining her there.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty days after her April marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Walter married Timothy Bishop on April 3, 1800 and died on May 3, 1800 at age nineteen — about thirty days after the marriage, with no children. The compressed marriage-and-death cycle within a single calendar month was striking even by the period's standards of vulnerability to acute disease. Her husband Timothy lived another seventy-three years and remarried twice.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa is buried at Grove Street Cemetery in New Haven, Plot 11 Cypress Avenue East. The cemetery — one of America's first planned burial grounds and a National Historic Landmark — became the family-burial cluster for the Walter-Pierpont line, with her parents William Jay Walter and Theodosia and several siblings later joining her there.

narrative_blocks.header: First wife died thirty days after wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Bishop's first wife Louisa Walter married him on April 3, 1800 and died on May 3, 1800 — about thirty days later, at age nineteen. Timothy was twenty-three at the time. He went on to remarry twice and live another seventy-three years, dying at age ninety-five.

Timothy Bishop was a successful New Haven merchant and member of the Congregational Church. He served as a bank director at age ninety-one — still active in business governance four years before his death. The late-life professional engagement was characteristic of a Connecticut merchant patriarch who outlived three wives.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Louisa died thirty days after marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia's daughter Louisa Walter married Timothy Bishop on April 3, 1800 — and died on May 3, 1800, just thirty days after the marriage. Louisa was twenty. The compressed marriage-and-death cycle within a single calendar month captured the period's vulnerability to acute disease across all socioeconomic groups, even within the Pierpont-Walter household at New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter named Theodocia Pierpont in her honor
narrative_blocks.body: A generation after Theodosia's 1806 death, her son William Walter's daughter named her own daughter Theodocia Pierpont Walter Hayden (born August 20, 1817) — honoring the Pierpont great-grandmother through both first name and middle name. The naming carried the Theodosia-and-Pierpont identity forward across three generations of women in the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson William Walter Hayden drowned in childhood
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia's great-grandson William Walter Hayden — son of Theodocia Pierpont Walter Hayden — drowned on August 2, 1850 in Windsor Connecticut at age twelve. The cascading family losses — daughter at twenty in 1800, great-granddaughter at twenty in 1838, great-great-grandson at twelve in 1850 — touched four consecutive generations of the Walter-Hayden line.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Haven 1758, died New Haven 1806
narrative_blocks.body: Theodosia Pierpont was born February 22, 1758 in North Haven Connecticut and died July 15, 1806 in New Haven Connecticut at age forty-eight. She married William Jay Walter on November 17, 1777 in North East Village, New Haven — he survived her by eighteen years, dying April 19, 1824. Her five documented children were Joel, Louisa, Lovina, Jerry, and William Jr.

bio.bio_blurb: Brick maker, Episcopal founder, warden fifty years

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Royalist Episcopal church at North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: He was a founder and the first senior warden of St. John's Church of England in North Haven, elected at age thirty-two in 1761. The rector and majority of members were Royalists during the Revolution but accepted the new government afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Senior warden for fifty years until death
narrative_blocks.body: He continued as senior warden of St. John's, with few exceptions, for fifty years — until his death in 1820 at age ninety-one. He outlived his wife Elizabeth Frost by sixteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Brick maker who served the Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: His occupation was brick maker in North Haven. Despite his Episcopal church's Royalist majority, he served in the Revolution — a fact recorded on his gravestone in the Old Cemetery.
